speaker: PAINTER
text: As I took note of the place, it cannot be far

speaker: PAINTER
text: where he abides.

speaker: POET
text: What’s to be thought of him? Does the rumor

speaker: POET
text: hold for true that he’s so full of gold?

speaker: PAINTER
text: Certain. Alcibiades reports it. Phrynia and

speaker: PAINTER
text: Timandra had gold of him. He likewise enriched

speaker: PAINTER
text: poor straggling soldiers with great quantity. ’Tis

speaker: PAINTER
text: said he gave unto his steward a mighty sum.

speaker: POET
text: Then this breaking of his has been but a try for

speaker: POET
text: his friends?

speaker: PAINTER
text: Nothing else. You shall see him a palm in

speaker: PAINTER
text: Athens again, and flourish with the highest. Therefore

speaker: PAINTER
text: ’tis not amiss we tender our loves to him in

speaker: PAINTER
text: this supposed distress of his. It will show honestly

speaker: PAINTER
text: in us and is very likely to load our purposes with

speaker: PAINTER
text: what they travail for, if it be a just and true report

speaker: PAINTER
text: that goes of his having.

speaker: POET
text: What have you now to present unto him?

speaker: PAINTER
text: Nothing at this time but my visitation. Only I

speaker: PAINTER
text: will promise him an excellent piece.

speaker: POET
text: I must serve him so too—tell him of an intent

speaker: POET
text: that’s coming toward him.

speaker: PAINTER
text: Good as the best. Promising is the very air o’

speaker: PAINTER
text: th’ time; it opens the eyes of expectation. Performance

speaker: PAINTER
text: is ever the duller for his act, and but in the

speaker: PAINTER
text: plainer and simpler kind of people the deed of saying

speaker: PAINTER
text: is quite out of use. To promise is most courtly

speaker: PAINTER
text: and fashionable. Performance is a kind of will or

speaker: PAINTER
text: testament which argues a great sickness in his

speaker: PAINTER
text: judgment that makes it.

speaker: TIMON
text: Excellent workman! Thou canst not

speaker: TIMON
text: paint a man so bad as is thyself.

speaker: POET
text: I am thinking what I shall say I have provided

speaker: POET
text: for him. It must be a personating of himself, a

speaker: POET
text: satire against the softness of prosperity, with a discovery

speaker: POET
text: of the infinite flatteries that follow youth

speaker: POET
text: and opulency.

speaker: TIMON
text: Must thou needs stand for a villain in

speaker: TIMON
text: thine own work? Wilt thou whip thine own faults

speaker: TIMON
text: in other men? Do so. I have gold for thee.

speaker: POET
text: Nay, let’s seek him.

speaker: POET
text: Then do we sin against our own estate

speaker: POET
text: When we may profit meet and come too late.

speaker: PAINTER
text: True.

speaker: PAINTER
text: When the day serves, before black-cornered night,

speaker: PAINTER
text: Find what thou want’st by free and offered light.

speaker: PAINTER
text: Come.

speaker: TIMON
text: I’ll meet you at the turn. What a god’s gold

speaker: TIMON
text: That he is worshiped in a baser temple

speaker: TIMON
text: Than where swine feed!

speaker: TIMON
text: ’Tis thou that rigg’st the bark and plow’st the foam,

speaker: TIMON
text: Settlest admirèd reverence in a slave.

speaker: TIMON
text: To thee be worship, and thy saints for aye

speaker: TIMON
text: Be crowned with plagues, that thee alone obey!

speaker: TIMON
text: Fit I meet them.

speaker: POET
text: Hail, worthy Timon.

speaker: PAINTER
text: Our late noble master.

speaker: TIMON
text: Have I once lived to see two honest men?

speaker: POET
text: Sir,

speaker: POET
text: Having often of your open bounty tasted,

speaker: POET
text: Hearing you were retired, your friends fall’n off,

speaker: POET
text: Whose thankless natures—O, abhorrèd spirits!

speaker: POET
text: Not all the whips of heaven are large enough—

speaker: POET
text: What, to you,

speaker: POET
text: Whose starlike nobleness gave life and influence

speaker: POET
text: To their whole being? I am rapt and cannot cover

speaker: POET
text: The monstrous bulk of this ingratitude

speaker: POET
text: With any size of words.

speaker: TIMON
text: Let it go naked. Men may see ’t the better.

speaker: TIMON
text: You that are honest, by being what you are

speaker: TIMON
text: Make them best seen and known.

speaker: PAINTER
text: He and myself

speaker: PAINTER
text: Have travailed in the great shower of your gifts

speaker: PAINTER
text: And sweetly felt it.

speaker: TIMON
text: Ay, you are honest men.

speaker: PAINTER
text: We are hither come to offer you our service.

speaker: TIMON
text: Most honest men! Why, how shall I requite you?

speaker: TIMON
text: Can you eat roots and drink cold water? No?

speaker: BOTH
text: What we can do we’ll do to do you service.

speaker: TIMON
text: You’re honest men. You’ve heard that I have gold.

speaker: TIMON
text: I am sure you have. Speak truth. You’re honest men.

speaker: PAINTER
text: So it is said, my noble lord, but therefor

speaker: PAINTER
text: Came not my friend nor I.

speaker: TIMON
text:
Good honest men.
                 
                (
                To
                 
                the
                 
                Painter
                .
                )
               Thou draw’st a counterfeit

speaker: TIMON
text: Best in all Athens. Thou ’rt indeed the best.

speaker: TIMON
text: Thou counterfeit’st most lively.

speaker: PAINTER
text: So-so, my lord.

speaker: TIMON
text:
E’en so, sir, as I say.
                 
                (
                To
                 
                the
                 
                Poet
                .
                )
               And for thy fiction,

speaker: TIMON
text: Why, thy verse swells with stuff so fine and smooth

speaker: TIMON
text: That thou art even natural in thine art.

speaker: TIMON
text: But for all this, my honest-natured friends,

speaker: TIMON
text: I must needs say you have a little fault.

speaker: TIMON
text: Marry, ’tis not monstrous in you, neither wish I

speaker: TIMON
text: You take much pains to mend.

speaker: BOTH
text: Beseech your Honor

speaker: BOTH
text: To make it known to us.

speaker: TIMON
text: You’ll take it ill.

speaker: BOTH
text: Most thankfully, my lord.

speaker: TIMON
text: Will you indeed?

speaker: BOTH
text: Doubt it not, worthy lord.

speaker: TIMON
text: There’s never a one of you but trusts a knave

speaker: TIMON
text: That mightily deceives you.

speaker: BOTH
text: Do we, my lord?

speaker: TIMON
text: Ay, and you hear him cog, see him dissemble,

speaker: TIMON
text: Know his gross patchery, love him, feed him,

speaker: TIMON
text: Keep in your bosom. Yet remain assured

speaker: TIMON
text: That he’s a made-up villain.

speaker: PAINTER
text: I know none such, my lord.

speaker: POET
text: Nor I.

speaker: TIMON
text: Look you, I love you well. I’ll give you gold.

speaker: TIMON
text: Rid me these villains from your companies,

speaker: TIMON
text: Hang them or stab them, drown them in a draft,

speaker: TIMON
text: Confound them by some course, and come to me,

speaker: TIMON
text: I’ll give you gold enough.

speaker: BOTH
text: Name them, my lord, let ’s know them.

speaker: TIMON
text: You that way and you this, but two in company.

speaker: TIMON
text: Each man apart, all single and alone,

speaker: TIMON
text: Yet an archvillain keeps him company.

speaker: TIMON
text:
(
                To
                 
                one
                .
                )
                 
              If where thou art, two villains shall not be,

speaker: TIMON
text:
Come not near him.
                 
                (
                To
                 
                the
                 
                other
                .
                )
               If thou wouldst not reside

speaker: TIMON
text: But where one villain is, then him abandon.—

speaker: TIMON
text: Hence, pack. There’s gold. You came for gold, you slaves.

speaker: TIMON
text:
(
                To
                 
                one
                .
                )
                 
              You have work for me. There’s payment.Hence.

speaker: TIMON
text:
(
                To
                 
                the
                 
                other
                .
                )
                 
              You are an alchemist; make gold of that.

speaker: TIMON
text: Out, rascal dogs!

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: It is vain that you would speak with Timon,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: For he is set so only to himself

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: That nothing but himself which looks like man

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Is friendly with him.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Bring us to his cave.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: It is our part and promise to th’ Athenians

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: To speak with Timon.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: At all times alike

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Men are not still the same. ’Twas time and griefs

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: That framed him thus. Time, with his fairer hand

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Offering the fortunes of his former days,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: The former man may make him. Bring us to him,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: And chance it as it may.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Here is his cave.—

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Peace and content be here! Lord Timon! Timon!

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Look out, and speak to friends. Th’ Athenians

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: By two of their most reverend Senate greet thee.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Speak to them, noble Timon.

speaker: TIMON
text: Thou sun that comforts, burn!—Speak and be hanged!

speaker: TIMON
text: For each true word a blister, and each false

speaker: TIMON
text: Be as a cauterizing to the root o’ th’ tongue,

speaker: TIMON
text: Consuming it with speaking.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Worthy Timon—

speaker: TIMON
text: Of none but such as you, and you of Timon.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: The Senators of Athens greet thee, Timon.

speaker: TIMON
text: I thank them and would send them back the plague,

speaker: TIMON
text: Could I but catch it for them.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: O, forget

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: What we are sorry for ourselves in thee.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: The Senators with one consent of love

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Entreat thee back to Athens, who have thought

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: On special dignities which vacant lie

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: For thy best use and wearing.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: They confess

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Toward thee forgetfulness too general gross;

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Which now the public body, which doth seldom

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Play the recanter, feeling in itself

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: A lack of Timon’s aid, hath sense withal

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Of it own fall, restraining aid to Timon,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: And send forth us to make their sorrowed render,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Together with a recompense more fruitful

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Than their offense can weigh down by the dram—

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Ay, even such heaps and sums of love and wealth

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: As shall to thee blot out what wrongs were theirs

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: And write in thee the figures of their love,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Ever to read them thine.

speaker: TIMON
text: You witch me in it,

speaker: TIMON
text: Surprise me to the very brink of tears.

speaker: TIMON
text: Lend me a fool’s heart and a woman’s eyes,

speaker: TIMON
text: And I’ll beweep these comforts, worthy senators.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Therefore, so please thee to return with us

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: And of our Athens, thine and ours, to take

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: The captainship, thou shalt be met with thanks;

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Allowed with absolute power, and thy good name

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Live with authority. So soon we shall drive back

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Of Alcibiades th’ approaches wild,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Who like a boar too savage doth root up

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: His country’s peace.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: And shakes his threat’ning sword

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Against the walls of Athens.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Therefore, Timon—

speaker: TIMON
text: Well sir, I will. Therefore I will, sir, thus:

speaker: TIMON
text: If Alcibiades kill my countrymen,

speaker: TIMON
text: Let Alcibiades know this of Timon—

speaker: TIMON
text: That Timon cares not. But if he sack fair Athens

speaker: TIMON
text: And take our goodly agèd men by th’ beards,

speaker: TIMON
text: Giving our holy virgins to the stain

speaker: TIMON
text: Of contumelious, beastly, mad-brained war,

speaker: TIMON
text: Then let him know, and tell him Timon speaks it

speaker: TIMON
text: In pity of our agèd and our youth,

speaker: TIMON
text: I cannot choose but tell him that I care not,

speaker: TIMON
text: And let him take ’t at worst—for their knives care not,

speaker: TIMON
text: While you have throats to answer. For myself,

speaker: TIMON
text: There’s not a whittle in th’ unruly camp

speaker: TIMON
text: But I do prize it at my love before

speaker: TIMON
text: The reverend’st throat in Athens. So I leave you

speaker: TIMON
text: To the protection of the prosperous gods

speaker: TIMON
text: As thieves to keepers.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Stay not. All’s in vain.

speaker: TIMON
text: Why, I was writing of my epitaph.

speaker: TIMON
text: It will be seen tomorrow. My long sickness

speaker: TIMON
text: Of health and living now begins to mend,

speaker: TIMON
text: And nothing brings me all things. Go, live still.

speaker: TIMON
text: Be Alcibiades your plague, you his,

speaker: TIMON
text: And last so long enough!

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: We speak in vain.

speaker: TIMON
text: But yet I love my country and am not

speaker: TIMON
text: One that rejoices in the common wrack,

speaker: TIMON
text: As common bruit doth put it.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: That’s well spoke.

speaker: TIMON
text: Commend me to my loving countrymen.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: These words become your lips as they pass through them.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: And enter in our ears like great triumphers

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: In their applauding gates.

speaker: TIMON
text: Commend me to them

speaker: TIMON
text: And tell them that, to ease them of their griefs,

speaker: TIMON
text: Their fears of hostile strokes, their aches, losses,

speaker: TIMON
text: Their pangs of love, with other incident throes

speaker: TIMON
text: That nature’s fragile vessel doth sustain

speaker: TIMON
text: In life’s uncertain voyage, I will some kindness do them.

speaker: TIMON
text: I’ll teach them to prevent wild Alcibiades’ wrath.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: I like this well. He will return again.

speaker: TIMON
text: I have a tree, which grows here in my close,

speaker: TIMON
text: That mine own use invites me to cut down,

speaker: TIMON
text: And shortly must I fell it. Tell my friends,

speaker: TIMON
text: Tell Athens, in the sequence of degree

speaker: TIMON
text: From high to low throughout, that whoso please

speaker: TIMON
text: To stop affliction, let him take his haste,

speaker: TIMON
text: Come hither ere my tree hath felt the ax,

speaker: TIMON
text: And hang himself. I pray you, do my greeting.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Trouble him no further. Thus you still shall find him.

speaker: TIMON
text: Come not to me again, but say to Athens,

speaker: TIMON
text: Timon hath made his everlasting mansion

speaker: TIMON
text: Upon the beachèd verge of the salt flood,

speaker: TIMON
text: Who once a day with his embossèd froth

speaker: TIMON
text: The turbulent surge shall cover. Thither come

speaker: TIMON
text: And let my gravestone be your oracle.

speaker: TIMON
text: Lips, let four words go by and language end.

speaker: TIMON
text: What is amiss, plague and infection mend.

speaker: TIMON
text: Graves only be men’s works, and death their gain.

speaker: TIMON
text: Sun, hide thy beams. Timon hath done his reign.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: His discontents are unremovably

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: Coupled to nature.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: Our hope in him is dead. Let us return

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: And strain what other means is left unto us

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SENATOR
text: In our dear peril.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              SENATOR
text: It requires swift foot.
